explanation: The natural language states that whenever an alarm is generated by one DM, all other DMs in the network receive it within 1 time unit. In LTL, to state that an event (alarm_generated_dm1) causes another set of events (alarm_received_dm2, alarm_received_dm3, etc.) to happen within the current or next time point (i.e., 'within 1 time unit'), we use the 'F[0:1]' pattern. For all other DMs (assuming a 5-DM network), we have to say that at every point where 'alarm_generated_dm1' is true, 'alarm_received_dm2', 'alarm_received_dm3', 'alarm_received_dm4', and 'alarm_received_dm5' all occur at the same or next step. That is, 'G(alarm_generated_dm1 -> (F[0:1] alarm_received_dm2 & F[0:1] alarm_received_dm3 & F[0:1] alarm_received_dm4 & F[0:1] alarm_received_dm5))'. The property precisely guarantees that the alarm is received quickly by every other DM. There is no previous LTL property to compare with in this case.
output_LTL: G(alarm_generated_dm1 -> ( (alarm_received_dm2 | X alarm_received_dm2) & (alarm_received_dm3 | X alarm_received_dm3) & (alarm_received_dm4 | X alarm_received_dm4) & (alarm_received_dm5 | X alarm_received_dm5) ))

explanation: The natural language sentence states that whenever an alarm is generated by a DM (for example, DM1), all other DMs in the network (here, DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5) should receive the alarm within 1 time unit. In LTL, the best way to express "within 1 time unit" is to use the F[0:1] (eventually in the next state or now) operator. Here, the specification can be written as: globally, if the alarm is generated by DM1, then in the next time step (or in the current step), DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 will have received the alarm. This ensures immediate or one-step-delayed receipt, matching the requirement. The formula does not use numerical comparisons, and instead captures the real-time constraint via LTL's next operator and disjunction.
output_LTL: G(alarm_generated_dm1 -> (alarm_received_dm2 & alarm_received_dm3 & alarm_received_dm4 & alarm_received_dm5 | X(alarm_received_dm2 & alarm_received_dm3 & alarm_received_dm4 & alarm_received_dm5)))

explanation:
The input natural language states that when a DM generates an alarm signal, all other DMs in the network must receive this alarm within 1 time unit. Among the given atomic propositions, 'alarm_generated_dm1' asserts that DM1 generates the alarm, and 'alarm_received_dm2', 'alarm_received_dm3', 'alarm_received_dm4', 'alarm_received_dm5' state that DM2 through DM5 receive the alarm. Since we are limited to LTL and not allowed to refer explicitly to time steps numerically, we use the 'next' operator (X) to express 'within 1 time unit.' Thus, for each receiver DM, if 'alarm_generated_dm1' occurs now, then in the next time step at the latest, the alarm must have been received by that DM. We combine these requirements for all other DMs (DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5). The formula captures this requirement globally (G) for every occurrence.

The resulting LTL property is:
G (alarm_generated_dm1 -> (X alarm_received_dm2 & X alarm_received_dm3 & X alarm_received_dm4 & X alarm_received_dm5))

This ensures that whenever an alarm is generated by DM1, then DMs 2-5 will each receive the alarm in the next time step (which is the strictest meaning of 'within 1 time unit' in LTL with only 'X').
output_LTL: G (alarm_generated_dm1 -> (X alarm_received_dm2 & X alarm_received_dm3 & X alarm_received_dm4 & X alarm_received_dm5))
